title: Question: How to run hashicorp vault as rootless container
body:
Hallo.

I'm trying to get running the [Hashicorp Vault container](https://hub.docker.com/_/vault) as rootless. This container needs **IPC_LOCK** capability to avoid flushing secrets on the disk,

If I run the container as root:
```
podman run --cap-add=IPC_LOCK -e 'VAULT_LOCAL_CONFIG={"backend": {"file": {"path": "/vault/file"}}, "default_lease_ttl": "168h", "max_lease_ttl": "720h"}' vault server
```
Everything works well.

But when I try to run the same command as above as a non-root user I get error in the container:

> Error initializing core: Failed to lock memory: cannot allocate memory
> 
> This usually means that the mlock syscall is not available.
> Vault uses mlock to prevent memory from being swapped to
> disk. This requires root privileges as well as a machine
> that supports mlock.

I'm not so strong in Linux Capabilities and all things arount this topic. So  I can ask if is possible to get this scenario run as non-root user or not. And if it is possible, how can I get this? 

Thank you very much for help.

comments.body: Might well be something that can not be done as rootless.  But before we make that decision, could you try with --privileged flag in rootless mode.

I don't think IPC_LOCK in a user namespace can make any difference.

One thing you can tweak to allow mlock to unprivileged users is to change the setting for `ulimit -l`.  This must be done as root through the file `/etc/security/limits.conf` (look for memlock).

> Might well be something that can not be done as rootless. But before we make that decision, could you try with --privileged flag in rootless mode.

I have already tried, but same result - no mlock available even if --privilege flag has been used
Could you attempt to strace it to see the system call that is being blocked.

> /etc/security/limits.conf

Seems that adding these two lines to the limits.conf:

```
*                hard    memlock         -1
*                soft    memlock         -1
```

works :) Thank you for your advice !

> Could you attempt to strace it to see the system call that is being blocked.

I can try to do that, but how can i run the strace inside the container? 

Or what do you exactly mean? @rhatdan Can you give mi more straigforward instructions? Sorry for inconvenience..
If you got it to work, great no need to trace.  I was just thinking that this was blocked by linux capabilities, but it was blocked because of resource limits.
Would you be interested in writing up a quick blog on this?

> Would you be interested in writing up a quick blog on this?

Oh, probably i can do that if I find a while... Do you think on my blog or somewhere on podman site ? Anyway, the Vault is very interesting project and many othes can appreciate to run it as rootles, I think.

> Seems that adding these two lines to the limits.conf:
> 
> ```
> *                hard    memlock         -1
> *                soft    memlock         -1
> ```
> 
> works :) Thank you for your advice !

I'd suggest you set a limit, even if high but set one :-)  You probably don't want to allow unprivileged users to lock all the available memory
Yes we could publish the blog at podman.io, or at redhat.com/sysadmin potentially.

> > Seems that adding these two lines to the limits.conf:
> > ```
> > *                hard    memlock         -1
> > *                soft    memlock         -1
> > ```
> > 
> > 
> > works :) Thank you for your advice !
> 
> I'd suggest you set a limit, even if high but set one :-) You probably don't want to allow unprivileged users to lock all the available memory

You are reight. It hs been only quick test. I'm going to re-configure system for the vault with different settings. But as proof of concept, it has work with -1  :)
